plainMeaning: This verse counts the Jina temples at several towns — two at Sūrācanda, two at Chevaṭṭhāṇa, four at Bāhaḍameru — and then praises Lord Pārśva at Rāudaha.
recitation: sūrācaṁde dunniya dunniya chevaṭṭhaṇaṁmi jiṇa bhavaṇo, caüro bāhaḍamere pāsaṁ ca thuṇāmi rāudahe.

wordByWord.etymology: place-name Sūrācanda in the locative singular (-e, < Skt. loc. -e); the second member echoes candra ('moon')
wordByWord.notes: Identification of the site is uncertain; carried as a proper place-name.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit dunni / duṇṇi 'two' (< Skt. dvi-, cf. dvau/dve) + euphonic/connective -ya
wordByWord.notes: Reduplicated in the line (dunniya … dunniya): 'two … two', distributing the count over two towns.

wordByWord.gloss: two (the second 'two', for the next town)

wordByWord.etymology: place-name in the Prakrit locative -ṁmi (= Skt. loc.); final member ṭhāṇa < Skt. sthāna 'place, site'
wordByWord.notes: Read as a single place-name in the locative; the exact site is not firmly identified.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jiṇa < Skt. jina, 'conqueror', from √ji 'to conquer'
wordByWord.notes: Compounded with the following word: jiṇa-bhavaṇa, 'Jina temple'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bhavaṇa < Skt. bhavana 'abode, mansion'; nominative -o
wordByWord.notes: jiṇa bhavaṇo = 'Jina temples', the objects being counted across the towns.

wordByWord.etymology: place-name in the locative singular -e; final member -meru/-mera, a common western-Indian toponym element
wordByWord.notes: Possibly the Bāhaḍameru / Bahadmer (Barmer-region) of medieval western India; identification not certain.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pāsa < Skt. pārśva; accusative -aṁ, the object of 'I praise'
wordByWord.notes: Singled out for personal homage, distinct from the temples merely counted before.

wordByWord.gloss: I praise / I sing the praises of
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit thuṇāmi, 1st-person sg. present < Skt. √stu 'to praise, extol' (cf. staumi)
wordByWord.notes: The verse's one finite verb of homage; shifts the line from tallying temples to personal devotion.

wordByWord.gloss: at Rāudaha (a tīrtha by a lake)
wordByWord.etymology: place-name in the locative -e; final member daha < Skt. hrada 'lake, pool', a frequent toponym element (rāu- possibly < rāja-, 'royal')
wordByWord.notes: Read as the site where the praised Pārśva is enshrined; identification uncertain.

literalTranslation: At Sūrācanda two, two at Chevaṭṭhāṇa — Jina temples; four at Bāhaḍameru; and I praise Pārśva at Rāudaha.
idiomaticTranslation: Two Jina temples stand at Sūrācanda and two at Chevaṭṭhāṇa; four more at Bāhaḍameru; and at Rāudaha I bow in praise before Lord Pārśvanātha.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This is one of the hymn's pure roll-call verses: instead of telling a legend, the poet simply tallies how many Jina shrines stand at each town — two at Sūrācanda, two at Chevaṭṭhāṇa, four at Bāhaḍameru. The numerals do the devotional work; counting the temples (dunniya, dunniya, caüro) is itself an act of reverence, a 'census of devotion' that lets the reciter salute whole clusters of shrines in a single breath. The line then pivots. After three towns named only by their temple-counts, the verb thuṇāmi ('I praise') breaks in for the first time in the verse, and a single Jina — Pārśva at Rāudaha — is lifted out of the tally for personal homage. The movement is deliberate: from impersonal enumeration to the intimate first person, from 'how many temples' to 'whom I praise'. This rhythm sets the verse off from its neighbours, which lean on story — the preceding verse evokes Ṛṣabha at the Gufāgiri cave-hill of the Pārkar country, and the following one moves to Kannauj and the wooden Vīra image of Sacaur. Here, between two narrative verses, the poet gives a brisk geographic ledger capped by one focused bow, keeping the garland moving bead by bead.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Mūrtipūjaka conviction that a Jina image enshrined in a bhavaṇa is a true locus of veneration; therefore even to count the temples of a town is to honour the Jinas housed within them, and the merit of darśana is gathered without travel. The closing thuṇāmi addressed to Pārśva — the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara — enacts the hymn's governing gesture of homage on a named, well-loved Jina, reminding the reciter that the abstract tally finally resolves into devotion to a specific liberated soul worthy of praise.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, a worshipper mentally bows at each town in turn, venerating every shrine there at once; the shift into the first-person thuṇāmi invites the chanter to make Rāudaha's Pārśva a personal object of praise, turning a list of place-names into an act of present-tense devotion.

crossReferences.text: Logassa — praise of the 24 Tīrthaṅkaras, in which Pārśva (Pāsa) is named
crossReferences.source: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Tirthmalastava — a tīrthamālā of the same genre listing regional western-Indian tīrthas

sources.title: Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Hindi dictionary), H. D. T. Sheth
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit glosses for dunni ('two'), cauro ('four'), ṭhāṇa (< sthāna), bhavaṇa ('temple'), and thuṇāmi ('I praise')

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit–English Dictionary (Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots/etyma √stu ('to praise'), sthāna, bhavana, hrada ('lake'), pārśva and catur underlying the Prakrit forms

sources.title: Tirthmalastava — Book Summary, JainGPT
sources.consultedFor: The tīrthamālā genre and the Mahendrasūri lineage that frames such Prakrit pilgrimage roll-calls of western-Indian tīrthas

sources.title: Ancient Jaina Texts of History (PDF), Jainworld
sources.consultedFor: Medieval Jain tīrtha catalogues (incl. the '84 mahātīrtha' tradition) and the practice of enumerating regional shrines

notes: The Prakrit vocabulary is secure (dunni 'two', cauro 'four', ṭhāṇa < sthāna, locatives in -e / -ṁmi, jiṇa-bhavaṇa 'Jina temple', thuṇāmi < √stu 'I praise'). The place-names, however, are uncertain and reflect medieval western-Indian sacred geography: Sūrācanda, Chevaṭṭhāṇa (read as a single place ending in -ṭhāṇa < sthāna), Bāhaḍameru (possibly the Bahadmer/Barmer region) and Rāudaha (rāu-daha, perhaps a 'royal lake' site) were not firmly identified against an independent gazetteer and may carry variant readings in other editions. 'dunniya' is analysed as dunni + a euphonic/connective -ya. The syntactic distribution of the two 'dunni' counts and the 'cauro' across the named towns is the most natural reading but is not fixed by punctuation; the danda and verse-final period follow the source orthography.
